topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise celebration for Sarah has been cancelled." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, a clumsy paralysis setting in. I reread the message three times, the words blurring into a meaningless pattern. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, a physical weight that made each breath shallow. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop mirroring the sudden, cold emptiness inside me. The vibrant anticipation I had carefully nurtured for weeks evaporated, leaving behind only a desolate silence. I walked to the kitchen, the floorboards protesting under my weight, and stared at the half-packed decorations—balloons deflated, streamers limp. It felt like a physical blow, the sudden absence of the planned joy leaving a raw, exposed feeling.

[story 2]

I stared at the phone screen, the cheerful invitation text mocking me with its cheerful tone. Canceled. The word echoed in the quiet of my apartment, sharp and brittle. My hands began to tremble, the fine motor skills failing as a wave of profound disappointment washed over me. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees tight to my chest, trying to physically contain the churning in my stomach. Tears welled up, hot and unwelcome, blurring the edges of the room. Every memory of the planning—the whispered secrets, the carefully chosen outfits—now felt tainted, a cruel reminder of something beautiful that had been snatched away. I let out a small, choked sound, a sound more animal than human, and curled inward, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

When I finally read the notification, the air seemed to thin out. It wasn't a loud crash, just a slow, sickening drop in the atmosphere. I didn't move immediately; I just sat there, perfectly still, my gaze fixed on the screen as if expecting the text to change, to retract, to prove itself wrong. A strange numbness began to spread through my limbs, a heavy fog that made simple actions feel monumental. I stood up slowly, my movements stiff, and walked into the living room. The remnants of the setup—a half-inflated banner, a forgotten cake box—looked absurdly out of place now, relics of a future that had just been erased. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to scrub the image of the cancellation from my vision, but the feeling remained, a deep, unshakeable void where excitement used to reside.
